questions.question: How is trust built in romantic relationships?

options.text: Through promises and declarations ('Trust me!')
Through repeated demonstrations of behavioral consistency, availability, responsiveness over time (100+ instances of 'I needed them and they showed up')
Trust arrives spontaneously if you love someone enough
Length of time together automatically creates trust

questions.explanation: Trust = behavioral consistency over time, not feeling that arrives spontaneously. Built through: repeated experiences (100+ instances of 'needed them, they showed up'), behavioral consistency (partner does what they say), responsiveness to distress (partner notices and responds when struggling), repair after conflict (partner re-approaches, apologizes when wrong). NOT built through: promises/declarations, intensity of chemistry, length of time alone (years don't guarantee trust if behavior inconsistent). You can't will yourself to trust someone with inconsistent behavior — trust emerges from accumulated evidence.

questions.question: What is the difference between minor ruptures (normal, repairable) and major ruptures (require professional help)?

options.text: All ruptures are equal

Minor: forgotten dates, harsh words during conflict, temporary withdrawal — repairable with acknowledgment/commitment; Major: infidelity, significant lying, financial betrayal, broken major commitments — require couples therapy
Minor ruptures are more serious than major ones
Professional help is never needed

questions.explanation: Minor ruptures (normal, repairable with self-help): forgetting important date/event, harsh words during conflict, temporary emotional withdrawal, not following through on minor commitment. Repair: acknowledge harm, understand impact, behavioral commitment to change. Major ruptures (require professional help): infidelity (emotional/sexual affair), significant ongoing lying (finances, substance use, other relationships), betrayal of confidentiality, financial betrayal (hidden debt, gambling), broken major commitment (violating monogamy agreement, refusing therapy after agreeing). Minor ruptures erode trust incrementally; major ruptures shatter it acutely.

questions.question: Why is active infidelity crisis out of scope for self-guided relationship education?

options.text: Infidelity is easy to handle without professional help

Active affair involves acute emotional disruption, betrayal trauma requiring trauma-informed facilitation, high re-traumatization risk, complex stay-vs-leave decision-making — requires Gottman Method or EFT couples therapy
Infidelity never requires professional help
Self-guided courses can address all relationship issues

questions.explanation: Active infidelity crisis is out of scope because: acute emotional disruption prevents productive self-guided work, betrayed partner experiences betrayal trauma (intrusive thoughts, hypervigilance, emotional flooding) requiring trauma-informed facilitation, decision-making (stay vs. leave) requires neutral third party, high risk of re-traumatization without professional support. Evidence-based approaches: Gottman Method for affair recovery (pilot study d=1.14, large effect), EFT (addresses attachment injury from betrayal). Both require trained therapist, both partners present. Crisis resources if acute: 988 Lifeline, Crisis Text Line (HOME to 741741).

questions.question: What are the three phases of rebuilding trust after major rupture (with professional help)?

options.text: There are no phases — trust is impossible to rebuild

Phase 1: Stabilization (stop bleeding, establish safety, address trauma) Weeks 1-4; Phase 2: Understanding (context not excuse, address relationship issues, rebuild through consistency) Months 2-6; Phase 3: Recommitment or separation decision Months 6-12
Immediate forgiveness with no process
Trust rebuilds in 1 week

questions.explanation: Three phases with professional help: Phase 1 Stabilization (Weeks 1-4): Stop bleeding (affair contact ends, full disclosure), establish safety (no further lying, transparency), address betrayed partner's trauma (therapist validates, holds offending partner accountable). Phase 2 Understanding (Months 2-6): Understand context (NOT excuse but understand), address underlying relationship issues, begin rebuilding through behavioral consistency (offending partner: radical transparency, reliability; betrayed partner: process trauma, decide on reconciliation). Phase 3 Recommitment or Separation (Months 6-12): Has trust begun rebuilding? Sustained behavior change? Can betrayed partner envision future? → Recommitment or healthy separation.

questions.question: What are the steps to repair minor ruptures?

options.text: Ignore the rupture and hope it goes away

(1) Acknowledge harm without defensiveness ('I see I hurt you, I'm sorry'), (2) Understand impact on partner (ask how it affected them, listen without minimizing), (3) Behavioral commitment to change (specific actionable plan, then demonstrate through repeated behavior)
Blame partner for being too sensitive
Make vague promises with no follow-through

questions.explanation: Steps to repair minor ruptures: (1) Acknowledge harm without defensiveness — NOT 'You're overreacting' or 'What about when YOU...'; YES 'I see I hurt you, I'm sorry'; (2) Understand impact on partner — ask 'Help me understand how that affected you', listen without minimizing/justifying/counter-attacking; (3) Behavioral commitment to change — specific actionable commitment ('I'll set phone reminders for important dates' not vague 'I'll try to be better'), then demonstrate through repeated behavior (trust rebuilt through accumulated evidence, not promises). Defensiveness blocks repair — acknowledgment is prerequisite.

title: Trust, Reliability, and Repair After Rupture - Quiz
